user_prompt: when `Hotel` decides to shutdown, blank out the screen, draw `sprite_sleep_100` right in the center of the screen, wait 3 seconds, then shutdown. During this, `FlyGui` is not polling, nothing is polling or pumping.

the battery reading mechanism can issue a shutdown if the batter is too low, similarly blank out the screen, prevent any more polling from anything, draw `sprite_lowbatt_100` in the center of the screen
On boot, after `BattTracker::init();`, do one check of battery level to see if it needs to shutdown in the above way.
I would suggest the actual functionality of these be implemented in `lib\FlyGuiViews` under a file called `ShutdownView.cpp` but it doesn't have to be a class object, it's just like... 2 draw calls.

user_prompt: also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes
agent_reply: The common shape is now: constants/types/prototypes first, primary view/API methods next, supporting/drawing/formatting helpers later. I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.
- `src\version.c` build churn was restored; final status only shows the intended layout files.

user_prompt: is the time held until shutdown known or configurable?
agent_reply: Yes, but there are a few different “times” in play:
- M5Unified’s generic button hold threshold defaults to `500ms`.
- During M5 init, for PMIC-backed power buttons, it does:
- This is configurable in firmware with:
- But your app is not currently using `M5.BtnPWR.wasHold()` or `isHolding()` for shutdown.
- On the Core2 / AXP192 path, M5Unified config comments show the PMIC has a hardware key long-press setting:
- The local M5Unified init table sets AXP192 register `0x02` to `0b01101100`; based on the comment, that selects the `3s` long-press setting.
- That is PMIC-level behavior, not the same as the M5 button class threshold.
- It is technically configurable by writing the PMIC register, but not exposed by our app as a normal setting.
So it shows the shutdown icon for 3 seconds, then calls `M5.Power.powerOff()`.

So: the PMIC’s hardware long-hold-to-power behavior looks known and effectively set to about **3 seconds** on AXP192. The firmware button “hold” event is shorter, about **600ms**, and configurable with `M5.BtnPWR.setHoldThresh(...)`. The app’s own idle shutdown is **5 minutes**, plus a **3 second** shutdown display delay.

- The local M5Unified init table sets AXP192 register `0x02` to `0b01101100`; based on the comment, that selects the `3s` long-press setting.

So it shows the shutdown icon for 3 seconds, then calls `M5.Power.powerOff()`.

So: the PMIC’s hardware long-hold-to-power behavior looks known and effectively set to about **3 seconds** on AXP192. The firmware button “hold” event is shorter, about **600ms**, and configurable with `M5.BtnPWR.setHoldThresh(...)`. The app’s own idle shutdown is **5 minutes**, plus a **3 second** shutdown display delay.
